anchor_paper.why: This is the clearest high-signal anchor for Beat 1 because it consolidates the recursive-reuse literature into a widely recognized mixed empirical-theoretical statement: indiscriminate training on recursively generated data can cause irreversible collapse, especially through loss of distribution tails. It supports the beat's opening claim without forcing the stronger and unsupported claim that all synthetic data is harmful.

spine.role_in_narrative: Introduces the core collapse mechanism: recursive training on generated data makes models forget low-probability regions of the original distribution.
spine.transition_to_next: Follow-up work sharpens this into a more operational condition, arguing that the key danger is self-consuming training without enough fresh real data entering each generation.

spine.role_in_narrative: Extends the collapse story into the go-MAD framework, specifying that insufficient fresh real data leads to quality or diversity degradation across generations.
spine.transition_to_next: Taken together, these early results motivate a broader synthesis: recursive reuse is risky not as a slogan, but as a generational distribution-shift process.

spine.role_in_narrative: Serves as the beat's anchor by establishing collapse under recursively generated training data and foregrounding irreversible tail loss as the central failure mode.
spine.transition_to_next: Crucially, however, this literature mainly proves risk under indiscriminate recursive reuse, not universal synthetic-data failure; later work makes that scope limit explicit by showing stable mixed-data regimes.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the required counterpoint: mixed real-plus-synthetic training can avoid collapse, so the evidence supports a narrower warning about unmanaged recursive reuse rather than a blanket rejection of synthetic data.
spine.transition_to_next: Once narrowed in that way, the next question becomes empirical rather than theoretical: are real information environments accumulating enough synthetic content for recursive exposure to become a practical pretraining concern?

spine.role_in_narrative: Offers one of the strongest domain-specific measurements of contamination pressure by quantifying LLM usage in scientific writing, while still falling short of proving universal web-scale contamination.
spine.transition_to_next: Because these contamination measurements remain partial and environment-specific, the literature also asks whether reactive filtering can reliably keep synthetic text out of future corpora.

spine.role_in_narrative: Closes the chain on the limits of reactive filtering by showing that many detector families can be broken under recursive paraphrasing, making post hoc cleanup fragile rather than impossible.
spine.transition_to_next: Overall, the strongest Beat 1 conclusion is conditional: recursive synthetic reuse is a real pretraining risk, contamination pressure is increasingly plausible but not yet universally proven, and detector-based filtering is an unstable last line of defense.

supporting.role: Shows a self-correcting loop can stabilize self-consuming training, reinforcing the scope limit that collapse is avoidable under corrective mechanisms.
Provides a curated synthetic-data success case in which self-consuming training converges toward preference-optimal distributions rather than degenerating.
Preliminary web-scale measurement suggesting AI content is widespread in new webpages, though usually mixed with human text rather than purely synthetic.
Adds a volume-based signal that AI-generated articles may now exceed human-written ones in raw output, while also noting search-ranked content remains mostly human.
Translates contamination pressure into downstream system risk by showing how AI-polluted web content can degrade retrieval diversity and quality.
Represents the optimistic filtering baseline through watermarking, which later adversarial work tests and partially undermines.
Shows paraphrasing can evade detectors while preserving a narrower positive claim that retrieval-based defenses may still help in some settings.
Provides low-effort real-world bypass evidence, underscoring that detector fragility is not limited to sophisticated attacks.

paragraph_outline.topic: Recursive synthetic reuse can cause collapse, but the evidence supports a narrower claim about indiscriminate reuse rather than universal synthetic-data failure.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Recent work has made a strong case that recursively training models on their own outputs can collapse the learned distribution, especially by erasing rare but important tails, while also showing that this risk is conditional rather than universal.

paragraph_outline.topic: Evidence that contamination pressure is building in information environments is emerging, but it remains partial, measurement-specific, and not yet a proof of universal web-scale contamination.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The bridge from collapse theory to current pretraining risk is more tentative: available studies indicate rising exposure to machine-generated content in scientific writing and parts of the web, but the evidence is still domain-specific and incomplete.

paragraph_outline.topic: Reactive filtering is an important mitigation idea, but the detector literature shows that it is brittle under paraphrase and simple evasion tactics.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: A natural response to contamination pressure is to detect and filter AI-generated text after the fact, yet the strongest detector studies suggest this defense is only partially reliable once adversaries or even routine rewriting enter the loop.

writing_notes: Keep the beat explicitly three-step: collapse risk -> tentative contamination-pressure evidence -> reactive filtering limits. Make the scope admission unavoidable before any category-B bridge: the literature strongly supports risk from indiscriminate recursive reuse, not the claim that all synthetic data is harmful. Name the counterevidence directly: pi^2/6 mixed real+synthetic pathway, self-correcting loops, and curated synthetic-data regimes. For category B, use cautious language such as 'emerging', 'domain-specific', and 'not yet universal proof'; avoid saying the web is definitively contaminated at training-relevant scale. For category C, avoid saying detection is impossible; the stronger claim is that watermarking and other detectors are fragile under paraphrase or low-effort manipulation, even if retrieval or provenance-aware defenses can still help in narrower settings. Do not import any fine-tuning evidence from categories F, I, or J.

anchor_paper.why: It is the strongest direct longitudinal content study in the set: a large, curated 25-year web genre analysis that shows measurable drift while remaining appropriately domain-bounded rather than claiming web-wide contamination.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes the measurement caution: corpus-level diversity or complexity signals can be tracked, but metric choice is sensitive to sampling and corpus size, so any drift claim should begin with bounded proxies rather than sweeping web-wide conclusions.
spine.transition_to_next: Because the corpus contains no post-2022 web-scale contamination audit, the literature moves from abstract proxy robustness to longitudinal web archives where change can be observed directly within a stable document genre.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides a concrete long-span web measurement case by curating a million-document privacy-policy corpus and showing systematic temporal drift in length, readability, and ambiguity.
spine.transition_to_next: Extending this curated privacy-policy line, later work deepens the content analysis and shows that the drift is not only stylistic but also substantive.

spine.role_in_narrative: Strengthens the longitudinal evidence with a 1996-2021 content analysis showing increasing use of location data, implicit collection, limited user choice, and growing third-party sharing; this is the beat's anchor example of measurable web drift.
spine.transition_to_next: Taken together, these genre-specific findings motivate a broader question: do similarly large shifts appear in the web's overall sectoral composition and platform ecology?

spine.role_in_narrative: Widens the frame from one document genre to 25+ years of web evolution, showing shifts in dominant site types and platform composition across the web archive.
spine.transition_to_next: But longitudinal web evolution studies still do not quantify synthetic contamination rates, so the narrative turns to crawl-construction work that inspects present-day pretraining corpora and tests whether filtered web data remains usable.

spine.role_in_narrative: Supplies the required counterevidence: with careful filtering and deduplication, web-only data can still train strong LLMs, so observed drift is not decisive proof that web corpora are already unusable.
spine.transition_to_next: Building on this web-only curation result, later work scales the pipeline across many Common Crawl snapshots and again reports strong performance, reinforcing the need for a narrow conclusion.

spine.role_in_narrative: Closes on curation practice rather than proof of failure: systematic large-scale filtering over 96 Common Crawl snapshots yields better-performing pretraining data, implying that measurable drift creates rising risk and curation pressure, not demonstrated web-wide degradation.
spine.transition_to_next: Therefore, the literature supports only a partial and indirect measurability claim: some drift and access changes are observable, but no post-2022 web-scale contamination audit in this corpus establishes web-wide pretraining degradation.

supporting.role: Early proxy-method support: shows entropy-based topic-model diagnostics can be principled, while underscoring that inference depends on metric design rather than directly observing contamination.
Adds a second measurement-proxy example via renormalization analysis of topic models, useful for framing proxy stability but still indirect with respect to web contamination.
Provides complementary evidence that online attention is increasingly concentrated among a small set of dominant domains, reinforcing structural drift in the web ecosystem.
Documents unexpected content, benchmark contamination, machine-generated text, and filtering trade-offs in C4, motivating why later web-only datasets emphasize more careful curation.

Shows rapid change in robots.txt and Terms-of-Service restrictions for major training corpora, linking web drift to data availability and legality rather than directly proving synthetic contamination.
Offers a bounded post-GPT signal that AI-generated text is rising in a specific high-visibility domain, useful as indirect risk evidence but not as a web-wide contamination estimate.

paragraph_outline.topic: Proxy metrics can register distributional change, but only indirectly and with substantial dependence on metric robustness.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Because no post-2022 web-scale contamination audit exists in the corpus, this section begins with measurement proxies: work on corpus complexity, entropy, and topic-structure diagnostics shows that distributional change can be tracked, but only through metrics whose stability and interpretability must be handled carefully.

paragraph_outline.topic: Longitudinal archive studies show clear web drift in bounded domains and broad platform composition, without directly measuring web-wide synthetic contamination.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: More concrete evidence comes from longitudinal web observations: privacy-policy archives and long-range web-history analyses show that web content and attention patterns have shifted substantially over time, but these studies remain domain-specific or structural rather than direct contamination audits.

paragraph_outline.topic: Crawl-curation studies reveal present-day risk signals, yet also show that filtered web corpora still train strong models.
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Current pretraining-corpus work sharpens the picture but keeps the conclusion narrow: corpus audits find artifacts, governance constraints, and bounded signs of AI-text growth, while RefinedWeb and FineWeb demonstrate that carefully filtered web data still supports strong language models.

writing_notes: Keep the claim explicitly narrow. This beat is about partial measurability of drift, not demonstrated web-wide contamination or failure. State plainly that no post-2022 web-scale contamination audit exists in the corpus. Use the D-category papers only to motivate why proxies are fragile and indirect. Then pivot to H-category longitudinal studies as the strongest direct evidence of change over time, especially the privacy-policy line and web-evolution archive work. In the final paragraph, juxtapose risk signals (C4 artifacts, shrinking data commons, Wikipedia AI-content growth) with required counterevidence (RefinedWeb and FineWeb), so the conclusion is: drift and curation pressure are measurable in parts, risk is rising, but filtered web corpora still produce strong models and the literature here does not prove web-wide pretraining degradation.

anchor_paper.why: It is the clearest bridge paper for this beat: it connects synthetic-data provenance to measured declines in lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity, linking collapse-style concerns to the metric ingredients later assembled into L_auth.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes the provenance-side motivation for D1 by showing that recursive training on generated data removes tail support and reduces diversity across generations.
spine.transition_to_next: If recursive synthetic reuse can erase distributional tails, the next question is how that loss appears in observable language statistics rather than only in abstract collapse arguments.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the main empirical bridge from provenance to outcomes by showing that synthetic retraining is accompanied by declines in lexical, syntactic, and semantic diversity.
spine.transition_to_next: Once diversity decline is visible empirically, the literature turns to a harder measurement question: which indices are stable enough to summarize that change without being dominated by corpus-size artifacts?

spine.role_in_narrative: Grounds D2 metric choice by showing that common corpus complexity measures vary in robustness, motivating careful use of lexical-diversity indicators rather than naive reliance on raw TTR-style counts.
spine.transition_to_next: This caution about lexical metrics motivates adding entropy-based summaries, so that L_auth does not depend on a single family of diversity statistics.

spine.role_in_narrative: Supports D3 by treating Renyi entropy as an informative structural statistic, showing that entropy can track meaningful organization changes even if the setting is topic modeling rather than language-model pretraining.
spine.transition_to_next: From there, the key issue is how entropy relates back to lexical-diversity measures, since L_auth includes both and must avoid pretending they are fully independent.

spine.role_in_narrative: Links D2 and D3 by showing a consistent empirical and analytic relation between word entropy and type-token ratio, supporting their joint inclusion while also signaling potential overlap and the need for future calibration.
spine.transition_to_next: Taken together, these papers justify L_auth as a grounded synthesis of provenance and diversity ingredients, but not yet as a calibrated standalone law.

supporting.role: Provides a high-visibility empirical confirmation that recursive training on generated data causes irreversible loss of tail information, reinforcing the need for a provenance-sensitive input dimension.
Frames the same basic risk as Model Autophagy Disorder and emphasizes that fresh real data is required across generations, aligning naturally with D1 as a provenance ratio rather than a binary label.
Makes the real-versus-synthetic proportion explicit in theoretical terms, offering the closest direct analogue to D1 among the selected papers.

Adds theoretical support that entropy-like summaries can reveal stable structure across topic-granularity changes, strengthening the case for entropy as a descriptive ingredient rather than a one-off metric.

Shows that curation by human preferences changes the behavior of self-consuming loops, suggesting that provenance is not only about source fraction but also about socially shaped selection; this is a weak but relevant precursor to D4.
Provides a conceptual account of output homogenization and averaging, useful as motivation for a social-behavioral diversity dimension even though it does not operationalize D4 directly.

Extends the homogenization concern to human-facing behavior, arguing that generic outputs can diverge from human sensibilities; this is best used as scope-setting support for D4, not as hard validation.

paragraph_outline.topic: Why L_auth needs a provenance input dimension (D1)
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Recursive-training studies make clear that any descriptive framework for synthetic-data effects must track provenance explicitly, because the fraction of fresh human data versus model-generated reuse changes whether distributions remain stable or collapse.

paragraph_outline.topic: From provenance shifts to measurable outcome dimensions (D2 lexical diversity, D3 entropy)
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The main bridge to L_auth comes from work showing that synthetic retraining is associated with observable diversity decline, together with metric papers clarifying which lexical and entropy-based summaries are robust enough to describe that shift.

paragraph_outline.topic: D4 as a motivated but thinner dimension, and the scope limits of L_auth
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: A smaller and more conceptual literature suggests that provenance also affects social or behavioral variety, but this dimension is currently less directly operationalized than lexical diversity or entropy and should be presented as a motivated design input rather than a settled metric.

writing_notes: Frame L_auth as a stage-agnostic descriptive synthesis, not a discovered law. Make the architecture explicit: D1 Provenance Ratio and D4 Social Behavioral Diversity are upstream design-level inputs; D2 Lexical Diversity and D3 Entropy are downstream measurable outcomes. The strongest evidence chain in the selected papers is D1 to D2/D3, especially via The Curious Decline of Linguistic Diversity. The evidence base for D4 is materially thinner and more conceptual, so say that openly rather than overstating it. Do not describe L_auth as a detection tool. Also note that D2 and D3 are related rather than fully independent, with the entropy-TTR paper implying possible redundancy; this is exactly why weight calibration should be stated as future work rather than implied to be solved already.

beat_name: Fine-tuning Data Source Affects Social Reasoning

anchor_paper.why: Anchor paper for Beat 4: LIMA shows that a small, carefully curated human-authored set can drive large alignment gains, making data provenance a high-leverage post-training variable.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes why social reasoning is the right downstream target: even strong LLMs lag on mental-state and norm-sensitive tasks, so this capability cannot be assumed from pretraining alone.
spine.transition_to_next: Once social reasoning is established as a persistent weak spot, the next question is whether carefully curated post-training data can move that behavior more efficiently than scale alone. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Anchor paper for Beat 4: LIMA shows that a small, carefully curated human-authored set can drive large alignment gains, making data provenance a high-leverage post-training variable.
spine.transition_to_next: After LIMA makes curation a plausible treatment variable, the literature asks whether provenance alters socially meaningful outputs rather than only general chat quality. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Shows that post-training data source changes socially meaningful outputs, not just generic helpfulness: instruction tuning shifts whose opinions the model appears to reflect.
spine.transition_to_next: If provenance affects social judgments, then the optimization pipeline itself matters, which is why later work isolates a simpler preference-learning objective. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the clean methodological hinge: DPO simplifies preference optimization so feedback-source comparisons become more interpretable than full RLHF stacks.
spine.transition_to_next: With a simpler alignment method in place, the strongest counterclaim is that AI-generated preference signals may substitute for human judgments on bounded tasks. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides required counterevidence: AI feedback can match RLHF on bounded dialogue and summarization tasks, so the claim must stay narrower than universal human-data superiority.

supporting.role: Benchmark anchor showing that social interaction reasoning remains far below human performance.
Human-generated conversation corpus that makes post-training provenance concrete rather than hypothetical.
Low-cost simulated-feedback baseline that narrows any claim of uniquely human preference data.
Additional counterevidence that AI-feedback distillation can be highly competitive on general chat alignment.
Updates the theory-of-mind picture with a broader battery, showing newer models improve on some tests while still failing on faux pas-style social understanding.
Extends social-reasoning evaluation to open-ended responses and shows remaining gaps relative to humans, even with prompt-based improvements.
Qualifies human-preference pipelines by showing that socially agreeable responses can become sycophantic, so human data is valuable but not automatically normatively correct.
Adds evidence that self-generated instruction data can substantially improve general instruction following without relying on large new human annotation efforts.

paragraph_outline.topic: Social reasoning remains a distinct post-training weak spot
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The fine-tuning line should begin from task sensitivity, not from collapse theory: social commonsense and theory-of-mind style reasoning remain meaningfully weaker than generic instruction following.

paragraph_outline.topic: Curated human data and provenance shape socially meaningful behavior
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: Against that backdrop, human conversational corpora and curated instruction sets make data provenance concrete, and LIMA plus opinion-shift evidence show that source quality affects socially meaningful outputs.

The honest conclusion comes only after the counterexamples: DPO makes source comparisons cleaner, but AlpacaFarm, RLAIF, and Zephyr all show that synthetic or AI-feedback pipelines can succeed on bounded alignment objectives.

writing_notes: Keep Beat 4 clearly separate from collapse literature. The defensible chain is: social reasoning is a real post-training weak spot; curated human data can be disproportionately high leverage; provenance changes socially meaningful outputs; and bounded-task counterevidence prevents any universal claim that human data always wins. The final sentence of the beat should explicitly narrow the thesis to socially grounded, norm-sensitive behavior rather than alignment overall.

anchor_paper.why: Anchor paper for Beat 5: LIMA is the clearest precedent that data quality and curation can matter enough to justify a provenance-sensitive fine-tuning contrast.

spine.role_in_narrative: Establishes Social IQa as a demanding evaluation target, which is why the pilot uses social reasoning rather than only generic chat benchmarks.
spine.transition_to_next: Because the benchmark is intentionally socially grounded, the next step is a precedent showing that carefully curated fine-tuning data can materially change behavior. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Anchor paper for Beat 5: LIMA is the clearest precedent that data quality and curation can matter enough to justify a provenance-sensitive fine-tuning contrast.
spine.transition_to_next: Once data quality is treated as the intervention, the method must stay simple enough that condition-to-condition differences remain interpretable. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Defines the pilot's methodological hinge: DPO keeps the optimization recipe simple enough that differences between data conditions remain interpretable.
spine.transition_to_next: With a controlled preference objective in place, the strongest next baseline is Zephyr, which applies AI-feedback distillation on top of a DPO-style alignment recipe. [verified citation order]
spine.ordering_note: Verified by check_citations.py: Zephyr cites DPO.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the strongest competitive synthetic baseline: AI-feedback distillation can produce strong chat alignment, which is why the pilot must be framed as a directional test rather than a foregone human-data victory.

supporting.role: Broader theory-of-mind benchmark evidence showing that social reasoning deficits persist in strong LLMs.
Low-cost simulated-feedback baseline that makes a non-human comparison arm realistic for pilot studies.
Bounded-task counterevidence showing AI feedback can already match RLHF in dialogue and summarization.
Updates the social-reasoning story with a broader ToM battery, showing improvements in stronger models but also benchmark-specific weaknesses that keep evaluation nontrivial.
Extends the limitation to open-ended settings, reinforcing that social reasoning gaps remain even when closed-form performance appears stronger.
Provides a contrasting data-generation route via self-generated instructions, helping frame the pilot's data-source manipulation as methodologically meaningful.
Offers a nearby ranking-based alternative to PPO-style RLHF, supporting the claim that simpler preference-learning objectives are viable for controlled experiments.
Supplies survey-level caution that feedback quality, interpretation, and scalability remain unresolved, preventing overstatement of any pilot result.

paragraph_outline.topic: Why the pilot uses social reasoning as its evaluation target
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The experiment beat should begin by justifying the evaluation target: social reasoning remains difficult enough that small changes in data provenance are more likely to surface here than on generic chat benchmarks.

paragraph_outline.topic: Why a provenance-sensitive contrast is methodologically plausible
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: LIMA motivates the treatment variable, DPO keeps the comparison interpretable, and AlpacaFarm shows why a synthetic baseline is methodologically reasonable even if it is not the same as socially grounded human data.

paragraph_outline.topic: Why the outcome must be framed as directional rather than validated
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The strongest existing counterevidence comes from bounded alignment tasks: RLAIF and Zephyr show that AI feedback can already be highly competitive, which is why the present experiment can only offer directional evidence on social reasoning rather than full validation.

writing_notes: Beat 5 must stay method-and-pilot framed. The clearest inspectable chain is benchmark sensitivity -> LIMA as quality-sensitive precedent -> DPO as controlled optimization method -> Zephyr as the strongest AI-feedback baseline, with AlpacaFarm and RLAIF used as supporting narrowing evidence rather than as extra spine detours. End on limitations, not victory language: a 3B model may not separate data conditions sharply, D1 and D4 co-vary in the design, and the strongest non-human baselines come from bounded alignment tasks rather than dedicated social-reasoning comparisons.

anchor_paper.why: This paper most directly frames the design requirements relevant to CampusGo by arguing that AI data authenticity, consent, and provenance are currently fragmented and need unified treatment. It motivates a platform proposal without implying that any platform solution, including CampusGo, is already validated.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the broad feasibility precedent: mobile sensing can capture rich human behavioral traces over time.
spine.transition_to_next: A general sensing precedent becomes relevant only after it is narrowed into a campus-specific behavioral setting. [verified citation order]

spine.role_in_narrative: Narrows that feasibility claim into the university setting by showing that continuous smartphone sensing can characterize student behavior in a campus environment.
spine.transition_to_next: Once campus collection looks feasible, the key question becomes not sensing alone but how authenticity, consent, and provenance should be represented for AI use. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Anchor paper for Beat 6: AI data authenticity, consent, and provenance are currently fragmented, so any collection platform needs these design requirements baked in from the start.
spine.transition_to_next: Once provenance requirements are explicit, the next question is whether intentionally collected human interaction data can actually flow into alignment pipelines; OpenAssistant provides that bridge. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Provides the bridge from collection to downstream use: intentionally gathered human conversations can become alignment corpora, so a CampusGo-style platform is not just sensing infrastructure but a potential data-generation pathway.
spine.transition_to_next: A usable human-data pipeline is still not enough unless contributors remain visible as ongoing stakeholders in reuse and curation decisions. [thematic progression; no verified internal citation edge]
spine.ordering_note: No direct internal citation edge was found; order is justified by the argument progression rather than by a verified citation chain.

spine.role_in_narrative: Adds the stewardship boundary: contributors must remain stakeholders in how sensitive data are curated and reused, which keeps CampusGo proposal-framed rather than extractive.

supporting.role: Power-sensitive governance lens reinforcing that open-data defaults are insufficient for contributor-originated data.
Operational reminder that documentation, interoperability, and reuse norms often break before raw data collection does.
Adds a biomedicine precedent showing that harmonization and sharing involve information-loss trade-offs that simple openness frameworks do not settle.
Offers a lifecycle-documentation precedent from health data use, suggesting the importance of traceable stewardship for downstream reuse.
Suggests how community-governed platforms can track versioning and adaptation history, relevant to provenance-aware data infrastructures.
Supports the claim that existing privacy-policy consent surfaces are opaque and weak foundations for meaningful permission.
Provides longitudinal confirmation that notice-and-choice mechanisms remain limited despite regulatory changes.
Adds AI-specific evidence that permissions in the data commons are unstable over time, strengthening the case for ongoing provenance and consent tracking.

paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: The proposal should start with feasibility only: adjacent mobile-sensing work shows that campus-scale behavioral collection is technically plausible, not that it is automatically appropriate for AI training data.

paragraph_outline.topic: AI-specific provenance requirements define the proposal core
paragraph_outline.opening_sentence: What turns simple sensing into a defensible proposal is the provenance layer plus a downstream use case: authenticity, consent, and governance must be designed together, and OpenAssistant shows that intentionally collected human conversations can in fact become alignment data.
The final move is a scope limit: stewardship principles show why CampusGo can only be presented as a motivated design direction and not as a validated intervention.

writing_notes: Beat 6 should read as requirements engineering, not validation. Start from campus feasibility, pivot to the AI-specific provenance/authenticity problem, and then use OpenAssistant as the concrete bridge showing that intentionally collected human interactions can become alignment corpora. End on governance and stewardship constraints. Do not claim that any existing paper proves CampusGo will improve downstream models; the literature only motivates what such a system would need to satisfy.
